hypothesis: If E2EFOLD is trained end-to-end with a composite loss that jointly optimizes structure prediction and structure-based sequence design (including FAPE, Cα-FAPE, torsion, distogram, and sequence recovery losses), then the designed sequences will have higher sequence recovery and the corresponding structures will exhibit higher TM-scores and lower RMSDs compared with Boltz-2 designs and non-end-to-end baselines on benchmarks such as CATH 4.2 and E2EData, because the loss terms directly align sequence fidelity with accurate all-atom structure reconstruction.
